commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Chicken noodle scoop  (5)
2. Sticks around for a demo?  (3)
3. Hurricane or mudslide  (5)
4. Tanning target  (4)
5. 5/8 or 2/14  (4)


LADLE
TNT
DRINK
PELT
VDAY
What, they couldn't find a way to make ET TU BRUTE a theme answer?
Great theme, and pretty challenging!   Def don't expect to have to draw on what little Latin I know on a Monday!  Really liked the SUI GENERIS clue, such a great phrase.
Designer of this puzzle has a dark sense of humor joking about the threat of WWIII.
An agog capybara? I don’t think they do agog

What a delightful puzzle!  See how much Latin we all know?

Thank you Jamey!

So grateful that all these LATIN expressions are mundanely used in Portuguese! I might otherwise have been relieved that they're in a Monday puzzle with easy crosses. Huge shout-out to our very special and lovely (AD INFINITUM) in-house QUILTer, MOL, as well as our beloved and SUI GENERIS LATIN-LOVER, AD ABSURDUM! Thanking Mr. Smith for a fabulous Monday puzzle, I leave you all with Depeche Mode, with a song from their latest album, Memento Mori: https://youtu.be/2HHc8pNn7x8?si=wrV8EWyAMbqE80l1

What are U pods??
Have heard of K pods for coffee, never U pods.
Hmmm...
I loved the clue for AIOLI (12 down).

Wow! That was a Monday? A lot more resistance than the usual Monday!

I found 7D to be extremely flippant and tone deaf given the current state of the world.
Great puzzle, but with five long Latin phrases and other answers like OLLAS , I'm not sure I understand how this ended up on a Monday.
Today I learned that there is an alternative - and in my humble opinion far worse - way of spelling the word Epilogue.
Lots of Easter eggs for LATIN LOVERS in today's puzzle. NEBULA, ROMANO, even AIOLI and AGUA. And of course OVI which is really a META Easter egg.
Next time I’ll take Monday more seriously! Respect.

I did this puzzle with my mom today (after I did it), because she wanted to try it today. Neat puzzle! A couple of notes for today: 


1) The theme was just okay. Not mind-blowing, but not a dumpster fire. 
2) 49D was easily the weakest entry in the grid. There could've been some reconstruction there, especially when it was a rare (and, imo, worse) variant spelling.
3) 51A made me instantly think of this quote from "Yellow Submarine": "Ad loc, ad hoc, quid pro quo. So little time, so much to know!"

See ya! ;)
Well I’m glad they decided to bust out the Latin words on a Monday puzzle. That woulda been a nightmare later on in the week

Well, the Classics Minor is finally getting some use this weekend!
New PB for a Mon- or any-day, and by almost a full two minutes! (And here I must ask--does anyone else have times listed on their stats page, which are wildly inaccurate? Three-minute Saturdays, and the like? Possibly the result of beginning the puzzle on one devise, and finishing it on another? I don't count those.)

A couple days ago, I saw a pair of beavers swimming around the lagoon on Detroit's Belle Isle. And on the riverwalk along the Detroit River (technically the William G. Milliken State Park and Harbor), in a small wetland near, but not flowing into, the river, beavers have appeared to have built a lodge. They've also felled about half the trees around the pond, which didn't have that many to begin with. From the remnants scattered around the entrance to the lodge, the beavers have a fondness for Labatt's Blue and Sutter's Home single-serving bottles.

In fact, Amy, I don’t think OLLA is in Sam Ezersky’s Spelling Bee list. I think I tried it once in the past couple weeks to no avail. (OLLAS would probably not be, because the letter S is generally avoided.)

Record setting Monday natics.

Not my favorite.

Past two days have been 90% easy and punishing to finish. 

At least the comment button works now on the new in-app column page.
Will be great when it respects dark mode instead of face blasting me with white light at night.

I'm sick and grumpy and this puzzle wasn't the right one for me today.
Carpe diem! Thank you!
This is maybe my favorite Monday puzzle - not hard, but not a long list of stale clues. I enjoyed seeing whether I could come up with the theme answers with no or only a couple of crosses. Great fun!
A very enjoyable Monday!

One and done. No lookups. Whee...

Cogito ergo sum!!
A real Tuesday of a Monday with this theme

I can’t believe I had to use the puzzle check on a Monday. (INAPT, not INePT, d’oh!) The rest of it took a bit longer than usual, too. Is this the NYT way of shutting us up on all the “too easy” talk? If so,  I’m all for it.
7-down is a bit intense for a random Monday given the US escalation in Iran.
Fun puzzle. Imagine most vets will agree this was a Tuesday, not a Monday, though enjoyable just the same. Some original cluing too which is a bonus for early week. Thumbs up!

Had to think rather than recall. 👍🙂🙏

A worthy Monday. Mostly straightforward answers with original cluing.

Not knowing enough towns in Maine and ignorance of hammer nomenclature was almost my downfall. 

Thanks

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
If ever there was a Tuesday of a Monday, this was it.

Thanks for the non-sleepwalking solve, Jamey.
Today’s theme had me thinking about that classic Latin phrase:  SEMPER UBI SUB UBI.
Great Monday puzzle! Thanks Jamey and NYT.
Hecka hard for Monday. Though I am comfortable solving up to Wednesday so I mostly managed to finish, with the exception of needing the N in PEEN / ORONO which is just obscure knowledge frankly.

I am curious about NEBULA, which wasn’t included as one for LATIN LOVERS even though it is a Latin word (meaning cloud). 

POST MORTEM is what we Brits normally say instead of autopsy. The former is Latin (“after death “) and the latter Greek (“see for yourself”). Both are part of forensic pathology, which itself mixes Latin roots (forensic: to do with the court, or forum) and Greek roots (pathos + logos). 

It took me a while to get to the answer - i was thinking “post hoc”, “a posteriori”, even “ex post facto”.
imagine my shock solving the crossword in the bomb shelter and reading the 7A drone's sound and 7D hypothetical global conflict clues :) aced it

The column photo brought back a couple of memories.

Several summers ago while swimming in the pond in our neighborhood, we apparently got a little too close to our resident beaver's home.  We saw a little pair of eyes swimming right at us.  Respectfully, we turned around and started swimming away.  We swam for a bit, turned around, and the beaver was right on our tails.  We picked up the pace, checked again, and it looked like we had made no headway. (Cue the "Jaws" theme).  It wasn't until we were a good quarter of a mile away, that we saw that she had turned back.

Last year, we were out on our canoe on a full moon night.  Again we must have gotten closer than they wanted us to be.  First, we heard the cannonball like explosions of their tails hitting the water.  Then we heard them rubbing up against the underside of our aluminum canoe.  

Territorial little critters!

Spelling not my strong suit so having to come up with Latin words on a Monday after staying up late for the Oscars made a tough Monday for me. Enjoyed the puzzle, but I had to do a POSTMORTEM on my poor Monday puzzle time.

This was a bit too difficult for a Monday puzzle. Often folks will tell me they're not smart enough for crosswords. We all know that's because they think all puzzles are like The Times Saturday puzzles. I always tell them to start with the Monday puzzle and more times than not they end up enjoying crosswords. 

This puzzle was fun for a slightly experienced Tuesday player but it's not going to win over any newbies.

Lovely echo to yesterday’s masterpiece: Jamey’s first NYT published puzzle happened on the Ides of March (the 15th), albeit seven years ago.

I never thought about it, but I guess I am a lover of Latin phrases from the simple de facto, status quo, and ad hoc, to the lengthier sine qua non, ad hominem, and deus ex machina.  So, this theme gave me pleasure.

I liked the play on LATIN LOVERS too.

It’s a quirky theme – Latin phrases.  The theme of Jamey’s last puzzle (3/29/22) was quirky as well – metonyms.  I, a fan of quirky, loved this aspect of both.

Finally, I like how the puzzle didn’t autofill as Mondays sometimes can, that is, my brain experienced some carpe diem and cogito ergo sum.

Thus, a fun an engaging spark to the week.  Thank you for this, Jamey!

I've seen plenty of evidence of beavers (gnawed trees, etc.), but only one live, actual beaver. It was swimming parallel to the shore as my ex and I were taking a walk along Lake Monona in Madison, WI. We thought it might be an overgrown muskrat, because we have a lot of those--if only we could see the tail. (Muskrats have regular rat tails.) Finally the tireless guy did us the favor of flipping over and changing directions so we could get a good look at it. Beaver tail, all right.

I can see from the photo here that their noses are a lot bigger than a muskrat's, too. They're both a treat to see.

27D. It's a coin toss whether I get the spelling of the salts or the printer brand correct.
As soon as I realized the longer clues were Latin words I lost interest. Not my favorite but appreciate that it was unique

RE: 7D
Dear NYT, 
Stop the (keyboard) Presses is an actual thing 
OOF
Tough gig for those of us solving outside the US
Bit rough for a Monday, I thought.
Latin on a Monday?  Really?

I can understand why many newer solvers found this puzzle too hard for a Monday. Seems that it was much easier for us long-time solvers, thanks to a few old crossword answers like ORONO and OLLAS, along with the accumulation of years of general knowledge that's not filled with Star Wars, video games, and current slang.  I breezed through it, starting from 1A until I got to the end. I never took Latin in school but have been a LOVER of LATIN phrases since I got hooked on the first one I learned, when I was a kid. And as a Spanish major and once-fluent speaker of Italian, plus short stints as a medical transcriptionist, the phrases have always sorta rolled off my tongue. 

But when I got down near the end and simply filled in the few missing letters for LATIN LOVERS, I couldn't understand what connected those phrases with the revealer. Until I realized that I hadn't even bothered to read the clue!

Took me longer than usual for a Monday, and felt a bit more challenging as well. 

PUTSOFF rather than PUNTSON, and ALSOI instead of ASDOI slowed me down.

Five thematic entries and a revealer today, and still the fill is quite solid (except, perhaps, for MOPER). And BADPR is an interesting letter combination.

Nice start to the week!
I solved today's puzzle .9845319% faster than usual. New record!
I believe this may outrank all of the Mondays I’ve encountered for the 15+ years I’ve been attempting to solve NYT puzzles! Great theme and very nice cluing. Hope to see more Mondays of this caliber!
If you're not too worn out from this one, Patrick Berry has a nice one today at The New Yorker... https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/03/16

Here is my POST MORTEM. I thought this was the perfect follow up to yesterday’s puzzle. I liked the fresh clueing and the fill. My favorite was QUID PRO QUO not only for the Qs, but it was one of the things I would investigate when I worked in HR. I solved the grid just slightly slower than my average. Thank  you, Jamey. 

Carpe diem, Crosslandia!

Dear Amy Virshup, thanks for travelling to this part of the world!

FWIW I've entered OLLA in Spelling Bee many times, but it's never been accepted. Maybe one day. But I doubt we'll ever see OLLAS accepted.

Really enjoyed this! Sometimes Mondays can feel like a typing speed test, but today was more satisfying. I didn't know ORONO or OLLAS, and there was enough ambiguity to require consulting the crosses in several places on the grid. Thank you, Jamey!

What a great revealer for what is a very sophisticated Monday theme.  This is perhaps the first Monday puzzle I've ever seen where I think that if I'd had this puzzle idea myself, I would have leaped out of bed shouting "Eureka!"

Of course I would have expected it to be slotted for a Tuesday.  But if this is where Monday puzzles are heading, I'm certainly not complaining.

Question:  Are these Latin phrases familiar to every English-speaking person from every sort of background?  I wonder.  For me, three are phrases I've used often myself in casual conversation.  AD INFINITUM and POST MORTEM and QUID PRO QUO are useful phrases for me.  MAGNUM OPUS is a bit ironic, perhaps leaning slightly towards pretentiousness?  Nonetheless, I've said it.   The fourth, SUI GENERIS, I have never used in conversation or writing or in anything at all.  

I think there will be people who will complain the puzzle is too hard or even unfair.  But you won't hear that from me.  I loved it!!
Nice way to start the week.  A fairly quick, fun puzzle, and no look-ups today!  Did not know OLLAS, but the crosses confirmed it.
More interesting & engaging than typical Monday. Loved the theme, but especially loved the clue for obit pages.

It's hard to get excited by a Monday puzzle, but appealing to my love of the Classical world is a pretty good start.

I liked all the Latin phrases and loved the revealer.  (A good revealer is also the secret to an exceptional early-week puzzle.)

The puzzle makes me think about how many Latin phrases there are in popular usage in modern English, and the many ways that Greek and Roman culture shaped Western Civilization.

I'll list some of my favorites Latin phrases that didn't make the puzzle (or the Wordplay column) and hope that others add their favorites in the Comments.

A priori, ad hoc, ad hominem, ad nauseam, alter ego, alma mater, bona fide, carpe diem, de facto, e pluribus unum, excelsior, exempli gratia, ex nihilo, habeas corpus, id est, mea culpa, memento mori, non sequitur, per capita, per diem, per se, post hoc ergo propter hoc, pro bono, semper fidelis, sine qua non, status quo, verbatim, vice versa,...

Did I leave out your favorite?  How many of these have you learned from doing crosswords?

And, for those who watched the Oscars last night, we'll always have "In Memoriam".
This one solves more like a Tuesday, which was fine... just unexpected.

This was an excellent puzzle, a genuine rarity for the NYT under the Trumpian guidance of Will Shortz. Sadly, it was definitely NOT a Monday puzzle, more like a Wednesday. Is there anything Will Shortz can do right?

As with my French...I never studied Latin in school, but one can't help picking up phrases (and using them.)  This puzzle doesn't mine the Latin-rich legal profession, but it was a fun solve...just Over Too Soon.  
Ah, well.  One finds ways to make do.  High winds, bitter chill, and an ominous wall cloud this morning; yesterday it was 82 degrees and clear as a bell, with a sky of brilliant blue.  Crazy days...

I am a LATINLOVER. Simply delighted with this puzzle! 'Tis a miserable, dark, rainy, windy Monday here in SE CT. Absolutely normal March weather. Wouldn't I just love to crawl back in bed and ditch work?!

Today’s poem made from words found in today's puzzle

forever

a/ a kind of great sleep
in a cave of water
in a cloud of jupiter
d/ a still flight
a path home
life a/ open now
as i die
Gratias ago Jamey! To tweak the Roman poet Catullus: Amo et amo!
Kind of hard if you’re not American…

Overnight, the weather went from the 90's to the 30's. Woke up to a blue-norther migraine. That's my excuse for taking more than 15 minutes to complete a Monday puzzle. Got it in the end. Very clever. Sui generis.
Harder than normal for a Monday, but completely doable. I loved it.

This!

"One of the things I love about doing the puzzles is that they force me to accept when I am wrong, correct my errors and move on. I can’t dig in my heels and insist my answer to, say 7A, is right if the fill tells me otherwise. I’m not sure whether this has seeped into the rest of my life, but I like to believe it has. My loved ones may tell you otherwise."

Thank you for writing this so plainly, Amy.
What happened to easy mondays for beginners? I’m still learning and I look forward to mondays. NYT editors….. don’t forget….. beginners pay your subscription also. Give us one day please!

A marvelous Monday puzzle, maybe a MAGNUM OPUS even.

The Latin phrases were all known to me, common in American English, though I have never studied Latin nor worked in a Latin-heavy field.

I was taken aback by 7A/D, the drone sounds of global conflict, but y'all have addressed that. Too apt, rather than INAPT.

Awaiting storm front this afternoon.

I am tempted to complain to Jamey, but it is not her/his choice to assign a day. This is NOT a Monday puzzle! Once I figured the theme, I did okay but up to that point I thought there was something wrong with me! Alas, thanks for a fun, well constructed puzzle!

Oddly enough I am headed to ORONO on Tuesday for what should be an awesome concert! 

Solas, with Moira Smiley -- anyone here seen them?
Great puzzle, a very nice surprise to find a Monday puzzle with so much going on! Must have been quite a challenge putting this together, well done!
Ball-peen hammer was new to me. So I learned something.
I was bummed when Jarritos didn’t fit 56D!

At first glance, it looks like I finally did it! I Finished the puzzle EXACTLY on my average solve time according to XWSTATS ("You solved this 0:00 (0.0%) faster than your average Monday time"). But on closer inspection it seems to all be a lie. I finished in 5:29, but my average solve time for Mondays on XWSTATS is 5:46. I also checked my stats for the past year (5:45) and the past 60 days (5:17). Can any XWSTATS gurus explain what's going on?
Never have I seen a spelling of epilogue that didn't include the -ue at the end! I had no idea there was an alternate spelling!
Very, very nice. Great start to the week.
My jaw dropped at OLLAS - so excited to see that the tap-water-sickness wasn't the only thing I brought back from my trip to Mexico last week!!
Not sure how a puzzle with major clues in Latin (a language I thought was dead) ended up being a Monday

Happy day...I thought I'd lost my modest 315-day streak due to a much-needed trip to the Kentucky Bourbon Trail yesterday. Was pleasantly surprised when I did yesterday's puzzle first and the streak remained intact!
As a classics major and former Latin teacher, I approve
Cute theme! 😊

Latin is a language, as dead as dead can be,
First it killed the Romans, and now it's killing me.

No really, no trouble with this one at all, although I was expecting 
"ora pro nobis" to make an appearance, as it's a crossword staple.

Carthago delenda est!

One of the most unusual puzzle finds I've ever encountered. Two puzzles - one a Wednesday from 1995 by Chet Currier and one from April 30, 2002 by John F. Hughes. They both had 4 theme answers and those answers were positioned in exactly the same place in both puzzles. And... those answers... The 1995 puzzle: SINEQUANON QUIDPROQUO NEPLUSULTRA NONSEQUITUR And the 2002 puzzle: SINEQUANON QUIDPROQUO NEPLUSULTRA ADINFINITUM So only one difference in the puzzles. Could that possibly be a coincidence? Can't help but wonder. Here are those two links: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/6/1995&g=59&d=A https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/30/2002&g=17&d=A I'm done. .....
cool
Loved this puzzle today!

Inappropriate theme for a Monday. If your shtick is going to be increasing difficulty with Monday as your entry-level day, theming your puzzle around Latin phrases is a boneheaded decision. Should have run on Tuesday.

On a personal note, I didn’t jive with this constructor at all. Didn’t care for the WWIII “joke.” It’s interesting how much we can pick up from people just by completing a puzzle. For me, I hope I never encounter this person in real life. 

Ta-ta!
Enjoyed this one.  Def could be a Monday but I liked the Latin.  Knew it would be a doosy 3 words in lol.

I’m having this puzzle framed, and hanging it on my wall as a diploma - just in case anyone ever questions my qualifications for doing crosswords. 

A nice puzzle, but the filler could have been a little more themed. Not even an appearance of  L’oreo Ipsum.
Hoc amo!
Not a proper Monday puzzle by any means. Way too difficult.

It was playful.  I needed some playful desperately.   

This made today not quite so grim.   

Thank you.
Like so many others said, fine puzzle but definitely not a Monday.

I don't normally say anything about days of the week but I am making an exception today to say that this one felt like the Mondays I find in the archives from say, I don't know, 2020 and earlier. 

And I dug it!!  Good puzzle 

It was still, in my opinion, completely doable with very fair crossings. I never studied Latin, but the themers all seem to be in the language. 

What I didn't love was the 7A and 7D crossing.  Not at all saying they shouldn't have been there , but it was jarring considering the circumstances we're living in. 

For some reason I always think the hammer is spelled p e i n. 

I'm having technical trouble leaving this comment, but I wanted to underscore what Captain Q wrote below when he quoted the columnist talking about how puzzles make you see that you might not be right and how that can affect life outside of puzzles. If you missed her comments, please check them out or Captain Q's below.

Great puzzle! I was very relieved to come to the comments and see many people found this more difficult than a usual Monday! Took me longer than average but much less time than when I first started out. :) Hang in there.
Illigitimati non carborundum.
woof. save the themes and latin for thursday!
This puzzle wasn't too difficult.  Hiwever, it felt overly ambitious for a Monday puzzle.
Some really good cluing here and I finally found out why I needed to take Latin (jk, every time I do a word puzzle I am reminded of that!)

Late puzzle find - kind of interesting. A Tuesday from December 15, 2009 by Steve Dobis. The leading theme answer: 17a -"Zealous sort whose schedule may include 27-, 50- and 64- across" EAGERBEAVER And then those 3 answers, which are just clued as "See 17-across." PLACESTOGO THINGSTODO PEOPLETOSEE What was rather surprising to me was that PLACESTOGO and PEOPLETOSEE were both making their one and only appearance in any puzzle. Hmmm... Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/15/2009&g=17&d=A ....

Welcome to the NYT Crossword Puzzle Complaint Hotline.

Please press "one" to say the puzzle was too difficult, or press "two" to say the puzzle was too easy.
Crunch crunch!! What an unexpected Monday surprise! Just a fantastic puzzle.
TIL that when you solve on computer, your streak will not continue if you're one day late... There goes my New Year's Resolution to keep the streak alive for 2026 :(

Worst Monday puzzle in ages. Theme was ridiculous for a Monday puzzle. Punts on? Seriously? Learn how to construct a solvable puzzle before trying to create a Monday because you're wasting everyone's time with this nonsense. Horrible. 0/10.
this shouldn’t have been a monday puzzle i don’t think many people are competent in latin ?!?! :( very sad feel quite a silly billy today

Well, well, all late comment readers:
Just now, on Jeopardy, in the category "Historic Statements," returning champion Jamie Ding replied to a question about Caesar crossing the Rubicon with, "What is 'Alea iacta est'?" 

Silence

"What is 'The die is cast'?"

"Correct!"

O tempora, o mores:-(

(An answer in another category was "What is multitasking?," which is what I'm doing right now.)
Honestly appalling that the NYT editors think it’s funny to make a joke about a potential WWIII in the middle of a war (or really, any time).

Badly played, NYT. WWIII crossing with a clue about drones? Really? I've been doing the crossword daily for almost five years (and hitting up the archives for extra crossword fun), and this has to tie your use of "Irish Car Bomb" a few years ago as one of the most tone-deaf and inappropriate entries ever.

Difficulty was fine (I'm pretty sure the crossword-solving population skews a bit more Latin-familiar than average) but a little more situational awareness wouldn't hurt.
Didn't love it, didn't hate it. The Latin a little tough for a Monday.

Yes this was not your typical Monday puzzle. How do we improve if we are constantly handed layups. The comments are brutal.  Mine took longer than usual like so many others and I’m fine with that. I got my mental gymnastics in for the day and had to work just a bit harder, making the music at the end that much more rewarding.

I'll say I felt this was a very fair puzzle. If you consistently do Mondays, you can do this one. Was it on the harder side for a Monday? Sure, but was it anything a Monday solver couldn't work through? Nope. Really enjoyed the theme.

We've had plenty of comments of the "too easy for a Friday/Saturday" type. I guess "too hard for a Monday" balances those out? The cruciverbal Goldilocks Zone for any particular day is evidently a contentious issue.
Olla or lola's is not accepted by Spelling Bee.

Considering all the twisted knickers over WWIII appearing in the puzzle, and the usual sophomoric "do better" type of comments, perhaps it is time to remind people about the puzzle timeline. 

This puzzle was probably constructed months ago, perhaps a year or more ago. 

It was probably edited soon thereafter. 

It was probably scheduled for publication several weeks ago, long before our brand new "war of choice" was initiated. 

Sure, you might expect the editors to go through every puzzle with a fine-toothed comb, right before the last possible kill date, to suss out anything -- ANYTHING -- that someone, somewhere might find offensive, so they can kill it and mess up their schedule. 

But even if they do this... and I don't think they do... surely sometimes something will get past. 

Seriously, folks. Get a grip!
Keep this guy doing Friday crosswords.

I thought this was a lovely Monday! 

Surprised to see so many people taking issue with the Latin - I think nearly all of these answers are common parlance. 

Also fun to see Baylor! Sic ‘em bears! 🤠🐻

I'm reading how easy this was for so many people. I guess I'm a dummy because I didn't get half of this. I don't speak Latin and I've never heard of most of these phrases I'm apparently supposed to know. I'm glad so many people enjoyed it, but I'm just not one of them.
Not that hard but the hardest Monday I've ever done. 2x longer than normal.

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[Top guns?]  (6)(7)
[They croak as soon as they grow up]  (9)


TSHIRT CANNONS
POLLIWOGS

@Lewis

Hard not to include “Series that has over 200 Emmy’s…and one Oscar”

SESAMESTREET

Sue,
If you didn't get it before, this is the perfect time to do so.

And I'm sorry about that, KAC.  You got me good!
... or, riffing off today's theme, mea culpa.

Doesn't fit the theme set, Steve (but I think you knew that), and Caitlin worked ET TU into yesterday's cutline. I thought it funny enough to follow up a CAESAR Sunday with a LATIN Monday.
Well played, editors. And well done, Jamey.

agreed about 7D

@Andrew @DW

Had the same initial thought about 7D but of course the puzzle was probably submitted months ago. Not sure how close to publication date any final editing happens though - perhaps any constructors or editors lurking here can weigh in
@Alexis I’m 99% sure that almost every crossword we see was submitted many months beforehand.

@Gavin

Well exactly, that’s my point

@DJ

All the Latin phrases I feel are in common enough usage by English speakers to make them fair game on a Monday. YMMV of course..

@DJ

OLLA is classic old crosswordese. Remember it, it will show up again.

@Maddy

Because the added value of the “ue” is what?
"What's past is prologue."
I remember my grandfather at dinner in the 1970 rather angrily telling us it was HArass, not haRASS. Language changes. Even spelling can change, although more reluctantly.

@Maddy

File that away with color/colour, aluminum/aluminium, tire/tyre, center/centre, check/cheque, -ize/-ise, “zee”/“zed”, …

@Mike

EXquisite example of purist pet peeve.  See also grimACE.

@Mike
My H.S. English teacher around the same time said it was HArass, too, along with DIH-ri-gible, and many others that have not stood the test of his time, brilliant as he was. I still say HA-rass.

@Alexis
Aluminum/aluminium and zee/zed are really in a separate category, since they're actually pronounced differently. The others are simply spelling variations.

@Maddy

I blame "catalog".

@Times Rita
But the displaced stresses also sound different, even though spelled identically. See laBORaTOry vs LABraTOry, ADvertISEment vs adVERtisement, etc.

@The X-Phile

Or "analog."  Or even "dialog."

@Bill
Tch! They were supposed to land under cover of darkness.

@SBK in TO
Invading from Canada, yes, smuggling in their cheap prescription drugs, socialized medicine, and Sara and Tegan mix tapes.
@Bill -- dam fine beaver update.

@Bill
No lie! A few years back, at some Canada-US hockey game, the fans were yelling trash-talk at each other. At one point, the Canadian fans were chanting, "We've got health care! We've got health care!"  😏

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

commentBody:
@Heidi

Considering that 90% of the “too easy” talk takes place Thursday-Saturday (with the odd bit of spillover to Wednesday/Sunday), I doubt it.

I think it has more to do with the challenge of calibrating a Monday to be sufficiently easy to be approachable for brand new solvers, but without going full-blown USA Today (i.e. keeping the theme and fill at least *somewhat* interesting).

It’s a pretty fine line to walk, and there’s not much margin for deviating in either direction…

@Heidi
Inept leads to 17A being clued as [Asner, Begley, Sheeran, Mr., etc.]

@Gallay

Eh, felt like a normal Monday to me. Could have also been run on a Tuesday, I guess, but the boundary between any two days is bound to be pretty blurry. In part because I don’t think the goal is to aim for the exact same level of difficulty on any given day, week after week…and in part because that would be impossible to achieve, even if it was the goal.

@Matt 

Really? It was quick-ish for me for a Monday.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

@Andrzej 

As it should be!! ☺️

commentBody:
@Andrzej
Lingering is one thing but a lurking Andrzej gives me pause. Glad to see you in any mode.

@Strudel Dad

You had me looking for my "haha" response button.
@max. Agree!

max,
PEEN is obscure knowledge?
No shop class for you?

@max Our toolbox had two hammers--a "regular", and a ball peen. I've known that word since childhood. Different wheelhouses.

I've spent enough time in New England to know that UMaine is in ORONO (FWIW Bowdoin's in Brunswick and Bates is in Lewiston). But even though you're not from there, keep ORONO in your pocket; like WACO it's useful in a grid.

@Barry Ancona

I think PEEN is reasonably obscure. You are unlikely to see one in use and if you buy it's most likely a claw hammer. Many people's experience with tools nowadays is the hex key in Ikea furniture kits.

@max
and ORONO is, alas, the OREO of college towns with all those vowels so just memorize it (along with AIOLI--did you know that one?)
@Elbridge Gerry it may be obscure in current daily use, but PEEN is a very common crossword answer.

@Petrol I agree. i didn't like the weirdly vague translation of POSTMORTEM. I suppose a death does qualify as "an event," and I suppose there are metaphorical deaths that merit analysis without being, you know, actual deaths? Still, I found the clue unjustifiably oblique, especially for a Monday. They might as well have gone with: "something one or more living beings might do, or not do, after somehing happens, or doesn't happen, depending upon circumstances."

@Petrol

I have only once seen POST MORTEM used as clued today: on a gaming forum, where somebody used corporate slang for the evaluation of the release of a new video game. I was very surprised - why would you use a phrase relating to death in any other context than a mortuary one? This particular Latin phrase is never used idiomatically in Polish, and I've never heard or seen it used here, at all.

@Andrzej
People sometimes say "No post mortems" when they don't want to discuss eg a play or film after watching it.

@Andrzej 

In the US, a post mortem is also the term used for a discussion/meeting done after an autopsy to discuss what led to that person's death and if there was any way care could have been improved. Still weird that it's used outside of situations involving death, but it made it's way into other uses from there.

@xys

I'm glad it did not where I live. That's taking language too far.

@Petrol

I've seen/heard the usage "POSTMORTEM analysis" many times, usually in reference to a losing player or team reviewing a just lost game seeking to explain the loss. Common enough in chess and American professional sports, for example.

@Petrol. I’ve heard it used for an in-house discussion of a theatrical production: mostly from the technical perspective, what didn’t work and how to change for next time.  I always thought it a bit morbid.

@Andrzej
weird. I can't find any post from @xys to which you could be replying.

The thing is, when I post in the android NYT Games app, I'm getting the old comment format, without nesting. In the browser app I get nesting. It's super weird. Xys's post here in the app is a reply to my post above. In the browser though it looks different. The IT team at the NYT are still as bad as always, apparently.
@Andrzej found it kthxbai.

@Petrol we used POST MORTEM for many years in the US corporate world to describe a review after project completion to go over what we did right and what we could do better next time. The term eventually fell out of favor for being…too morbid.

@xys
Somewhere in the last few days, I read in a novel about someone whose car had died. The owner had taken it apart and 'was conducting a furious post-mortem' on the components.

@Two Cents
Think about how often we refer to a failure as a death. Comics die when they don't get a laugh. A book with an incoherent plot may die in the middle.  The light dies at the end of the day.
@anya I thought these clues were at best INAPT, and most certainly insensitive to the suffering being inflicted on your country and other countries. Stay safe, sending love.

@anya
Wonderful to have a voice from Kyiv here. I hope you are keeping safe. Please spare a moment to post again when you can. Despite the craziness in other parts of the world, many continue to follow reports from your homeland and do what can be helpfully done.

@anya
Your comment makes the world real in a much needed way.  Our day-to-day lives continue, while yours is fractured with danger and turmoil.  You are really there, and not just a story on the news or TV, or any of the media sites we see, which don't resonate in the way your comment does.  You are part of our community, and we need to feel that personally.  

It should be a wake-up call that our country is engaged in a war where people are forced to shelter to stay alive, and that our allegiance should be with the people who share our values as citizens, not with indifferent millionaires who view the world as an opportunity to make "deals."  It's too easy to forget what it takes to be truly brave.

Be safe, and continue to be strong and resolved, and know that you have touched us.

@Nancy J.

Around 40 years ago, I was fishing alone in a canoe along a Mississippi River backwater on a quiet summer day.  All was silent until I was startled by a huge WHAP right behind me.   Apparently I had floated too close to a beaver's abode and he slapped his flat tail on the water to express his great displeasure.  I practically jumped out of the canoe.

@Don H

It definitely gets your attention!

@Jeff Z
I used to live across from a pond that had a muskrat in it. I got so excited every time I saw it. I named him Mr. Muskrat, because I am super creative.

@Katie I once saw a muskrat building its nest. It was a highlight of my life. A friend was visiting from out of town and shared my amazement.

You could do worse than Mr. Muskrat!
@Katie, are you possibly the ad executive who named Mr. Coffee and Mr. Plumber?

@Don H

You left out Mr. Clean.

@Steve L 

I found this of average Monday difficulty, even though I'm a very different solver than you are.

@Steve L I looked up Orono post-hoc. It has fewer inhabitants than the village that I grew up in. Have people in the western U.S. actually heard of this city? I've travelled through Maine twice on holiday - although that was when America was still an appealing travel destination, so it's some years ago.

The Latin phrases were easy... a ball-peen though, I thought that would be NSFW.

@Steve L

I loved this puzzle, even though I found it fairly easy.
But I have a good foundation in the Classics.
YMMV.

@Arie

Sometimes, small places have an importance disproportionate to their population.  University towns are often like that, in no small part because often, state universities were constructed where the land was central and cheap.

ORONO, Maine; Oxford, Mississippi (yes, they had the audacity to name it that); Oxford, Ohio (ditto on the audacity); State College, Pennsylvania; Ann Arbor, Michigan; Charlottesville, Virginia; Ithaca, New York, and thousands of others.

But ORONO is of special importance to cruciverbalists, since its friendly letters means it'll be in the puzzle more than other college towns (251 times to date).

@Call Me Al,
I used to make quite a bit of beer - especially during 2020/21 COVID pandemic - and I always used EPSOM salts as a water adjustment for the style of beer I usually made. To this day, I still want to end that word with a “n” instead of a “D”.  (Probably consumed too much of my own product)

@Long walks n sunsets

When I was a newspaper editor, we had some issues with our printer. I had to be on press to approve the pages, due to their having blankets (protecting the pages on the press) with holes in them, causing horrid smears of ink on the pages. 

It was with no little glee that I jumped up and yelled "Stop the Presses!" when the first horrible sheet came through. 

My rep, starting to fall asleep over his coffee, jerked awake, looked at me through half-lids, and said with a smile, "Just how many years have you waited to say that?"

@Marlene

Lol, great story!

@Tain
And inside.

@Willson

Nota bene!

@Barry Ancona
Thanks! I always forget to go check out their Monday puzzle, and I usually enjoy it when I do go there.

@Barry Ancona

Thanks. That was enjoyably challenging, akin to a proper NYT Friday, for me. I managed to finish without lookups despite not knowing the trivia, which always feels nice.

Andrzej,
Patrick Berry is a very talented constructor. You should try some of his puzzles in the NYT Archive. You can identify them on xwordinfo.com.

@Barry Ancona

I'll probably get my gluteus maximus handed to me but maybe I'll try...
@ad absurdum most definitely mumbled something about spelling bee while entering this answer; came here to reaffirm non-acceptance.

@Anthony
As Barry and others have said, keep both in your pocket--they'll be back.

@Nancy, yep - SUI GENERIS tripped me up. I hadn’t run across that. Should have been solvable with the crosses, but I drew a blank on 11D (not enough coffee?), and tried to make the themed ending GENEsIS.

Nancy,
I'm with you on SUI GENERIS.
I've read it and heard it, but never written or spoken it.

@Nancy

For a LATIN LOVER like me, SUI GENERIS is a lovely was of saying "one of a kind" or "unique" or, more literally, "a family unto itself".

@BobSolo sui generis is used in legal brief writing or oral argument to state that the facts at hand are unique and/or presented to the court for the first time. I doubt many people have heard or seen it used outside those circumstances.

@Barry Ancona
The review I recall for _Watership Down_ said that the book (highly praised) was SUI GENERIS.  Decades ago, but a pleasure to remember.

@Nancy
I wouldn't know how to use SUI GENERIS in a sentence, that's for sure, but I've seen it before. Resolved to look it up later, but never did.

@Nancy
The Duke Ellington translation of sui generis: beyond category.

@Nancy
I actually do use SUI GENERIS irl.

I had an architect friend who had read it enough to know what it meant, but had never heard it. He assumed it was French, and when he dropped into a conversation he pronounced it 
"zhon-REE".

My family were/are avid readers, and over the years there have been many words where we knew the meaning but mispronounced the word when using it in conversation. We keep a list, and with each new generation it's a badge of honor to get a spot on the list--it means you're reading at an advanced level. Some of my contributions were apostrophe, catastrophe, amalgam, haphazard, and cacophony.

@Nancy yes I didn’t know sui generis either so tried veneris since to see is venir in French, and since I didn’t know what letter kind of suits pilots wear. I’m bummed that a Monday got me but it did. Cool puzzle though. TIL
SUI GENERIS is probably the one I have encountered the most times of all today's themers. It's used ad nauseam in Polish legalese...

@Crevecoeur
Venir is "to come" in French. Voir is "to see".

@Barry Ancona

Anna's ample clothing designs are SUI GENERIS.

@SBK in TO

And "crevecoeur" is heartbreak.
@Nancy That’s the one I didn’t know, too! If I’d ever read it, I must’ve forgotten it.
Cave canem.
Caveat emptor.

@The X-Phile

Cogito ergo sum

Cum laude

Veni, vidi, vici

Et tu Brute?

And there are more. 

...

@The X-Phile

in vitro

@lucky13 

I was going to say DEGUSTIBUS...ETCETERA

@The X-Phile

Ad hominem.


Ok, I will.  


Homonym.
The inclusion of AGUA in the puzzle just made me think of the lovely AQUA VITAE.

@The X-Phile
quod erat demonstrandum

@The X-Phile
Mens rea reo in corpore.

@The X-Phile
Where a priori goes, a posteriori is sure to follow. ;-)
And then there's all the footnotes: idem, ibidem, opus citatus.
And of course, at the end of every ĺist, et cetera.

@Jim Murray
Holy cow.  Are you sure you're Canadian?

@Jim Murray

In what sense is Will Shortz "Trumpian"???
An authoritarian populist?

And then I thought, "Why do I care what someone like this thinks?"
You'll find that Will is well-loved and well-respected here in Crosslandia, and if you don't share that love and respect, you probably haven't been following him for very long.

@MOL
Let's not blame the entire country for the opinions of one ill-informed person,....
although I'm tempted to.

@Andrew

Touché.

@Mean Old Lady

I wonder too. I would have expected a “Sorry…” by now! 😉

@Jim Murray Good grief, the idea that Will Shortz has anything at all to do with Der Gropenfuhrer. Dunno, man, seems a bit like jealousy. And if you're having such a terrible time with the NYT crossword, seems like a skill issue to me.

@Jim Murray

Wow, you're really a nickname-for-Richard. Your characterization of Mr. Shortz is way off the mark and unfair.

@IndyM

I don't see any evidence in Jim's comment that indicates that he's rich.

Ryan,

I feel like these days, it’s hard either way.
@Matt Even as a non-beginner I found this more difficult than a typical Monday.
@Matt agreed! I’ve gotten to the point where Mondays are normally no look ups but this one was impossible.
@David Gropper 100% agree with you.  This is NOT a Monday puzzle
Well, let me rephrase that: Most of it *is* a Monday puzzle, but some of these seem too obscure for a Monday.  I did okay with it, but it felt more like a Wednesday or Thursday to me.

@Captain Quahog
My guy went last night, to Waldoboro. I am a Moira Smiley fan but couldn’t make it. Enjoy!

@Cat Lady Margaret - Thanks! I'm definitely looking forward to it. 

Was Moody's the venue? ;^)
@The X-Phile--both are common types of hammer for different uses, or did you mean as xword clues/answers?

@Two Cents
A claw hammer can both pound nails and (with some luck) pull out old nails from wood. The claw gets in the way around machinery like car or boat engines.  The rounded peen on the peen hammer works better there. The peen can also be used to shape metal but I don't know anybody who has done this.

@The X-Phile
As I said elsewhere, we had both in my childhood toolbox. The ball peen was smaller and thus easier/safer for the kids to use. It had a flat face on one side; in fact according to some sources "peen" is the word for the part opposite the face, so the claw is a peen. The ball peen was specifically meant for shaping metal, although I doubt we ever used it for that.
@The X-Phile It's not a case of replacement, any more than Phillips screwdrivers have replaced slotted ones. Different tools for different uses.

@Carl
Leading to one of my favourite words: planish. It looks like an incomplete word or a derivative because of the -ish ending. But it's entirely itself and refers to an ancient and beautiful element of craft.

@Dave Munger

I have a similar discrepancy. Puzzle page showing 1:13 slower than avg but 5:46 minus 4:44 equals 1:02.

@K
elsewhere I compared it to omelet. Over time letters deemed extraneous can get dropped. (Although I will admit that according to ngram EPILOGue is used far more often than EPILOG).

@Rosa
Rule #1 when visiting Mexico:
1. Don't drink the water

Rule #2 when visiting Mexico:
2. See rule #1

@Rosa
Oh, no! Get better soon.

@Rosa

The one and only time I went to Mexico was to Mexico City for a physics meeting.

I knew not to drink the water, but it never occurred to me not to eat anything what would have been washed in the water, i.e., lettuce.

So at the airport waiting for transportation to the hotel, I had something with lettuce in it.

A few days later I found out what Montezuma thought of my being there.

I then saw that those around me were extremely careful about what they ate.  Bananas, ok.  Apples, no.  Oranges, ok.  Grapes, no.

@Jerry
Or, from my experience, wet your toothbrush with the tap water.

@Jenea

Because every one of these Latin phrases is also used in English.

@Jenea
As others have noted, it must have been a close decision putting this on a Monday. But I'm glad they did. In XLandia, I'll always lean on the side of toughness. (In real life, I'm a cream puff. Shhh.)

@Steve L I’d heard every one commonly except SUIGENERIS… which also crossed with GSUIT. I had to go through the alphabet to figure out what kind of suit it was! Not a huge deal but startling on a Monday for sure, which I can usually knock out in five or six minutes.

@Greg B
Yes, as long as you do them in order, your streak will be maintained. BUT... there is a limit to how to many days behind you can get before the streak will reset, and as I recall it's only about two days (don't ask me how I know).

@Seward Parker You're pretty much correct--as far as I can tell, you have until the second puzzle after the one you missed becomes available, for instance, for Monday you have until the Wednesday puzzle goes up. But of course a puzzle goes up the night before the day it's named for, and the hour depends on which day of the week it is. I think I know this from the same experience you do.

@Seward Parker
Thank you for this information!

@Seward Parker
And if only this were true of Wordle as well!
@D You mean "jibe," In fact, you did "jive" the constructor. Delicious puzzle. Perfect for Monday.
@Bob This is and always will be my most favorite, favorite malapropism of all time. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5CXeHk9W-iw
@D It truly is interesting how few words can say so much about someone. As for hoping to never encounter certain people, the feeling is mutual. Yikes.
@Scott Back at ya! :)
@Bob Not at all perfect for Monday but keep deluding yourself.

@D I agree today’s puzzle felt more suitable for a Tuesday or even Wednesday. 

What I can’t agree on is the rest of your statement. If you think it’s inappropriate to hope for no WWIII in our lifetime, then one can only assume you’re actually hoping for it.
@Michael B.  It's a beaut. My favorite is still "exercising" demons. This continues to get by copy editors at respectable periodicals. One of life's little pleasures.

@D
Here’s a thought: back when I started doing the NYT crosswords, I would buy the actual newspaper, read the newspaper, and finish up with the crossword if I had time. I didn’t feel like it was my prerogative to judge that the crossword was too hard, or too easy, or just right; I did the crossword because I wanted to, if I had the time.  That approach to solving might be more agreeable to you.

@McT 

I hope tomorrow's puzzle is also terrific, but I also hope that It's not a grim day for you. I hope it'll be a good puzzle on top of a good day! ☺️
Franciscus cafearum facit aqua qua farcimina cocta est.

@Andrzej

Sed gaudeo te redisse.

@Francis 

What do we press to say that we thought it was juuuuuust right!?

@Francis 

I think it does happen more than you've noticed, certainly by me. ☺️

@HeathieJ

That is true.  I exaggerate for comedic effect.

HeathieJ,

5*
@Francis - Well played, Francis!

@Francis 

That's why I stuck with the Goldilocks theme! ☺️

@Bob Puzzle 

Not everybody's time. Many of us enjoyed it.

@Roberta

Why not make a joke?  

It's not like there's anything we can do about it.  We're only along for the ride.  It's not our country, it's not our lives to do with as we wish.  We live only to serve our billionaire overlords, to make them ever more properous.

Writing our congressmen doesn't work.  Writing our senators doesn't work.  Appealing to the judicial system doesn't work.  Praying doesn't work, and Lord knows I've tried.

We might as well laugh as we await our demise.

@Roberta 

It did give me pause, admittedly, but dark humor can be a very useful thing at times like these, so I also had to chuckle. I sincerely doubt the Times is taking what's going on lately in a bad way.

@HeathieJ 

That is to say that I don't believe the times is joking about this in a bad way. Voice to text...

@Roberta

There is almost never a time when the US isn’t in some kind of war. That’s true going all the way back to our founding.
@A - I'd forgotten "Irish car bomb." But, here, using a race-based joke (along with drones and war,) must make the writers and editors feel so ironic and hip.

@Fabe F

Spelling Bee doesn’t accept a lot of common everyday words.

@Captain Quahog

Plus the obvious fact that we've been talking about WWIII ever since the end of WWII.

@Joi 

Some puzzle are like that. Don't feel discouraged come up please. We all have our own wheel houses. And the more puzzles we do, the better we get. Please hang in there! ☺️
